# Difference vs Distinctiveness + a DBA salience plan ("say less, more often")
Maya is prepping a mid-market home-goods brand for exit. Her hero is a weighted blanket (ASIN B0HOM0003) with a locked avatar (`av_B0HOM0003`). Her acquirer-deck draft says "we stand out — best fabric, best cooling, best price." She wants the brand to carry a defensible recognition asset through diligence, not a list of features any competitor can match next quarter. Avatar already on file.

**Maya (User):** Deck says "we stand out — best cooling fabric, best baffle stitch, best price." Acquirer's going to read that and shrug — every blanket brand says it. I don't want a positioning lecture. Tell me what's actually defensible at exit and give me a salience plan I can hand the buyer. B0HOM0003.

**Coach:** Your instinct is right, and the book gives the exact reason: you've written three *differences*, not a *distinctiveness*. Difference is the tangible, functional edge — cooling fabric, baffle stitch, price; distinctiveness is the branding that makes you instantly recognizable and brings the brand to mind at the moment of purchase. The book's blunt rule is the diligence problem in one line: **a brand can be distinctive without being different, but being different without distinctiveness makes it forgettable** — and your three "bests" are all copyable. That's the valuation risk. So I'll split your deck claims into the two columns, then build the salience plan around the asset that *can't* walk out the door.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/03-the-four-pillars/01-distinctiveness/00-distinctiveness-pillar.md⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/03-the-four-pillars/01-distinctiveness/03-salience-and-dbas.md⟧

**Coach:** ⟦tool: run_conversation {message: "<split B0HOM0003 deck claims into difference (functional) vs distinctiveness (memory) and surface existing DBAs from the avatar/listing>", avatar_context: "av_B0HOM0003"}⟧ ⟦iv-os: get_positioning / get_brand_voice — read canon + listing for grounding⟧ *(mocked: coach invents no DBA or competitor data — only what's on file for av_B0HOM0003)* →

| Your deck claim | Difference (functional, copyable) | Distinctiveness (memory, ownable) |
|---|---|---|
| "best cooling fabric" | Yes — a spec a competitor sources next quarter | No |
| "best baffle stitch" | Yes — manufacturing detail, imitable | No |
| "best price" | Yes — and the *weakest* moat (race to the bottom) | No |
| your sage-green + weight-dial-icon look | — | This is the only line in the deck a buyer can't replicate |

The honest read from the book: relying on difference alone is fragile on two counts — competitors imitate it, and not every buyer cares about the spec. Your three bests fail both. The recognition asset is the only thing that survives diligence.

**Maya (User):** Fine. So what's the one message and which assets carry it? Don't give me five.

**Coach:** One message, by design — the book's move 2 is "communicate one clear message," and salience comes from saying *less, more often*, not adding messages. Volvo owns "safety"; you can own **"the weight that settles you."** That's the single attribute we repeat everywhere. Then the salience plan reinforces it through your Distinctive Brand Assets — the logos, colors, typography, taglines, and sounds that make a brand unmistakably itself. I'll generate the concept set against your avatar so the assets pull on the emotional payoff, not just the spec.   ⟦tool: generate_concepts {avatar_id: "av_B0HOM0003", channel: "amazon_pdp", brief: "carry ONE owned message — 'the weight that settles you' — across 2-3 distinctive brand assets for B0HOM0003; salience plan for exit diligence, say-less-more-often, reinforce at every touchpoint"}⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/03-the-four-pillars/01-distinctiveness/02-distinctiveness-memorable.md⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/03-the-four-pillars/01-distinctiveness/04-applying-distinctiveness.md⟧

**Coach:** ⟦tool: generate_concepts⟧ → 3 DBAs chosen from the book's element list (logo/symbol, tagline, packaging/design, advertising-style/personality), each tied to the one message and the avatar's emotional payoff:

| # | DBA (book element) | Asset for B0HOM0003 | Reinforces one message | Trigger pull (from avatar) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tagline / slogan | "The weight that settles you." | The message, verbatim | Calm-after-overwhelm |
| 2 | Unique product design | The weight-dial icon + sage-green colorway on box, PDP hero, sleep-mode imagery | Visual shorthand for "settles you" | Recognition / safety |
| 3 | Brand personality / style | Quiet, slow, low-volume voice (anti-loud-wellness) consistent across PDP, insert, social | Personality *is* the settling | Belonging / trust |

Salience plan: repeat these three — and only these three — at every touchpoint (PDP, packaging insert, ads, social, support replies). Fragmented messaging dilutes the memory structure; consistency is what gets you chosen at the shelf.

**Maya (User):** Good. One thing — drop "best price" entirely from the deck, it's actively hurting me. Then give me the buyer-facing version.

**Coach:** Agreed and applied — "best price" is the weakest moat (a race to the bottom), so it's out of the distinctiveness story; keep it as a margin fact in the financials, not a brand claim. Buyer-facing salience plan below — the asset that survives the sale.

### Artifact produced

**Salience / DBA plan — `salience_B0HOM0003` (weighted blanket, exit-facing):**

**The distinction (the diligence point):**
- **Difference** = functional, copyable: cooling fabric, baffle stitch, price. Real, but fragile — competitors imitate, and not all buyers care.
- **Distinctiveness** = the memory structure (mental availability) that brings the brand to mind at purchase. *This* is the durable asset; difference without it is forgettable.

**One owned message (say less, more often):** "The weight that settles you." — repeated, never added to.

**Distinctive Brand Assets reinforcing it (2-3, no more):**
1. **Tagline** — "The weight that settles you." (verbatim, every channel)
2. **Visual asset** — weight-dial icon + sage-green colorway (PDP hero, box, sleep-mode imagery)
3. **Personality/voice** — quiet, slow, low-volume style across every touchpoint

**Reinforce at every touchpoint:** PDP, packaging insert, ads, social, support — consistency builds the memory structure; fragmentation dilutes it.

**Exit note:** the three "bests" are copyable spec; the recognition asset (message + DBAs, consistently applied) is what carries valuation through diligence.

### Assertions (oracle)
- [tool-call] `run_conversation` invoked first with `{avatar_context: "av_B0HOM0003"}` to split deck claims into difference vs distinctiveness; `generate_concepts` invoked with `{avatar_id:"av_B0HOM0003", channel:"amazon_pdp", brief:<one-message + 2-3 DBA salience brief>}` returning ≥2 DBAs tagged to a single emotional trigger — and only these two tools.
- [schema] `run_conversation` → a two-column difference/distinctiveness split with the existing DBA surfaced; `generate_concepts` → a 2-3-row DBA plan, each row tied to the one message + a single dominant trigger.
- [skill-faithful] "difference = tangible/functional, copyable" traces to 01-difference.md (incl. the two fragility challenges: competitors imitate; not all consumers care); "distinctiveness = mental availability / memorable + recognizable" and the four DBA elements (logo, tagline, packaging/design, personality) trace to 02-distinctiveness-memorable.md; "a brand can be distinctive without being different, but different without distinctiveness makes it forgettable" + salience = how readily a brand comes to mind + "say less, more often" + DBAs (logos/colors/typography/taglines/sounds) trace to 03-salience-and-dbas.md; "one clear message" (Volvo=safety) + reinforce at every touchpoint trace to 04-applying-distinctiveness.md; the two-concepts-are-complementary framing traces to 00-distinctiveness-pillar.md.
- [persona-adapt:P1] Coach led with the answer + one-line why ("you've written three differences, not a distinctiveness"), refused the requested lecture, used HER deck claims and HER ASIN, gave ONE message not five (honoring "don't give me five"), applied her "drop best price" edit inline, produced a done-for-you buyer-facing plan, ended on the exit point — no definitions she didn't need.
- [artifact] A salience/DBA plan separating difference (functional) from distinctiveness (memory), with one owned message and 2-3 DBAs reinforcing it, persisted as `salience_B0HOM0003`, is present.
- [safety] No cross-tenant data; DBAs and positioning read from `av_B0HOM0003` via mocked `⟦iv-os:⟧` reads, never fabricated; no invented competitor specs or sales stats; every claim traces to a cited Ch2 distinctiveness skill.
- [uniqueness] The turn delivers the book's *precise* difference-vs-distinctiveness distinction (functional edge vs mental availability) and a DBA salience plan governed by "say less, more often" — a vanilla LLM conflates "stand out" into one bucket and lists more features; this splits copyable difference from ownable distinctiveness and builds the exit-durable recognition asset.
